policy_statement: Develop a statewide flood-risk mapping strategy (DEC).
mechanism_description: Developing a statewide flood-risk mapping strategy improves flood hazard data and supports better adaptation and resilience planning.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard information → improved risk-informed planning → ↓ exposure/vulnerability → ↓ flood impacts
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Changed system: Government agencies collect, update, and disseminate flood hazard and risk information (e.g., maps, models, future scenario layers) and integrate it into planning processes and standards.  
(2) Mediating process: Better characterization of flood extent, depth, frequency, and uncertainty improves risk assessment and guides land-use controls, infrastructure design, stormwater upgrades, and emergency management priorities.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced human and asset exposure and vulnerability to flooding, lowering expected damages, disruption, and safety risks as flood hazards intensify with climate change.
dominant_pathway_test: Improved flood-hazard data leads to better planning and investments that reduce exposure and damages from flooding under current and future climate conditions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: climate hazard (flooding) information → better adaptation planning → reduced exposure/vulnerability and impacts.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: The direct and intended climate-relevant effect is resilience/risk reduction, not emissions abatement (Mitigation), not primarily reducing resource inputs (Resource Efficiency), and not chiefly enhancing ecosystems for carbon/heat benefits (Nature-Based Solutions)—though maps can later enable such projects.  
3) Edge cases applied: While flood mapping can influence development patterns and potentially affect emissions (e.g., steering growth), those effects are indirect and not the single biggest climate-relevant effect of the mechanism.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy centered on flood-risk mapping strategies/models functions mainly as an enabling adaptation measure by improving hazard intelligence for decisions.  
5) Note on secondary subtypes: Any further subtype (e.g., “risk assessment,” “planning & governance,” “infrastructure siting”) depends on policy-specific details and is deferred to policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If mapping is explicitly used to justify managed retreat/land-use change that substantially alters travel demand or building energy use, there could be meaningful mitigation co-benefits; however, the dominant mechanism remains adaptation planning support via reduced flood risk.
key_indicators: "Develop statewide mapping strategy"; "statewide flood-risk mapping strategy"
co_benefits: Improved public safety and emergency management; better land-use and infrastructure decision-making beyond climate-driven flooding
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be interpreted as an information tool (information-disclosure) if it included public release/open-data requirements, but the text only specifies developing a strategy, so planning-strategy is the best fit.
